source_name: Uruguay — Operation Condor-era bans (Centre for Global Education + Brooklyn Rail)
_note: Two new UY entries documenting the 1973-1985 military dictatorship's systematic suppression of leftist literature. Uruguay's only existing UY ban (1 record) misses the most famous case — Galeano's Open Veins (which we have as a CL ban already, here adding UY as country of origin). Plus Benedetti's La Tregua / The Truce as cited example of his banned oeuvre.

entries.title: Open Veins of Latin America
entries.author: Eduardo Galeano
entries.description: Eduardo Galeano's 1971 history of European-American economic exploitation of Latin America from the conquest through the Cold War. Banned by Uruguay's military dictatorship (1973-1985), along with Argentina and Chile. Galeano himself was imprisoned by the Uruguayan military and forced into exile for eleven years (Argentina 1973-1976, then Spain after the Argentine coup). The book remained the definitive primer on Latin American dependency theory for decades.

entries.title: The Truce
entries.author: Mario Benedetti
entries.description: Mario Benedetti's 1960 epistolary novel about a Montevideo office clerk's brief romance — Uruguay's best-known 20th-century novel and an icon of Uruguayan literature, with over a million copies sold worldwide. After the June 1973 coup Benedetti's entire body of work was banned by the Uruguayan military dictatorship; he lived in exile (Argentina, Peru, Cuba, Spain) until the return of civilian government in 1985, when he co-founded Brecha alongside Juan Carlos Onetti and Eduardo Galeano.
